name: Stock Market
blurb: How markets really work, and how to read a stock.

lessons.title: What Actually Happens When You Buy One Share
lessons.concept:
When you tap "Buy" on Groww or Zerodha, you never meet a seller. Your order flies to an exchange — either the NSE (National Stock Exchange, home of the Nifty 50) or the BSE (Bombay Stock Exchange, Asia's oldest, founded 1875, home of the Sensex). There a computer called the order-matching engine pairs your buy with someone else's sell in microseconds, following strict price-time priority. To even take part you need three linked accounts: a trading account (to place orders), a demat account (to hold shares electronically, safe-kept by the depositories NSDL or CDSL), and a bank account (for money). Your broker is just a licensed gateway to the exchange, and the whole system is policed by the regulator SEBI.

The single most useful beginner skill is knowing your order types. A market order says "buy now at whatever the best available price is" — fast, but you don't control the price. A limit order says "buy only at ₹500 or better" — you control the price, but it may never execute. The gap between the highest price buyers are bidding and the lowest price sellers are asking is the bid-ask spread; in a liquid stock like Reliance it's a paisa or two, in a thinly-traded small-cap it can be several rupees. Rookies fire market orders into illiquid stocks and get filled at ugly prices — so prefer a limit order whenever a stock is thinly traded.

Two more essentials. India runs one of the fastest settlement cycles in the world: trades settle on a T+1 basis, meaning shares land in your demat and money leaves your account one working day after the trade (SEBI is even piloting an optional same-day T+0). And two words you'll hear daily: a bull believes prices will rise (a "bull market" is a sustained upswing), a bear believes they'll fall. These aren't predictions to blindly follow — they're just labels for mood.
lessons.example: You place a market order for 10 shares of a thinly-traded small-cap at 9:16 AM. The best seller has only 3 shares at ₹200; the next lot of 7 sits at ₹214. Your market order sweeps both, so your average buy price becomes ₹209.80 — nearly 5% above where you thought you were buying. Had you placed a limit order at ₹201, you'd have bought just the 3 shares and waited, protecting yourself from that jump.
lessons.code:
Market order for 10 shares eats the order book top-down:

   3 shares @ ₹200 = ₹600
   7 shares @ ₹214 = ₹1,498
  ------------------------------
  10 shares total  = ₹2,098

Average price = 2098 / 10 = ₹209.80 per share

A LIMIT order at ₹201 fills only the 3 shares @ ₹200 and
leaves the rest resting: you never overpay, but you may under-fill.
lessons.tryit: Open your broker app, pick any Nifty 50 stock, and view the "market depth"/order book (the 5 best bids and 5 best asks). Note the bid-ask spread in rupees. Then check a random small-cap and compare how much wider its spread is. Don't place any trade — just observe.

mistakes.m: The BSE and NSE are separate companies whose share prices differ, so I should buy wherever it's cheaper.
mistakes.fix: They are two exchanges listing the same companies; prices are near-identical and any gap is arbitraged away in milliseconds. Choose based on liquidity (usually NSE), not a hunt for a 'cheaper' price.

mistakes.m: Market orders are always best because they execute instantly.
mistakes.fix: Instant execution isn't free — on an illiquid stock a market order can fill far from the last price. Use a limit order whenever the spread is wide.

mistakes.m: Once I buy, the shares are mine to sell that very instant with no waiting involved.
mistakes.fix: You can sell intraday, but formal settlement is T+1 — the shares are officially credited to your demat the next working day.

links.t: Zerodha Varsity — Introduction to Stock Markets

quiz.q: What is the key difference between a market order and a limit order?
opts: A market order guarantees a price but not execution; a limit order guarantees execution but not price
A market order guarantees execution but not the price; a limit order controls the price but may not execute
Both guarantee price and execution; only the fees differ
A limit order can only be placed by institutions, not retail investors
quiz.why: A market order prioritises speed — it takes whatever price the order book offers, so execution is near-certain but the price is not. A limit order fixes your acceptable price, so you never overpay, but if no one meets your price it simply doesn't fill. Option 0 reverses the two.
quiz.approach: On liquid large-caps a market order is usually fine; on thinly-traded stocks, protect yourself with a limit order.

quiz.q: On the standard rolling cycle, when are Indian equity trades currently settled?
opts: T+5 (five working days later)
Instantly, within the same second
T+1 (one working day after the trade)
T+30, at month-end
quiz.why: India runs one of the world's fastest settlement cycles at T+1 — shares and money change hands one working day after the trade, with SEBI even piloting optional same-day T+0. T+5 was an old global norm years ago, not today's India.
quiz.approach: Know your settlement cycle so you understand exactly when shares land in your demat and when funds are debited.

quiz.q: Which entity actually holds your shares in electronic (dematerialised) form?
opts: Your bank
SEBI
The stockbroker's own personal account
A depository — NSDL or CDSL — via your demat account
quiz.why: Shares live electronically in a demat account, and the real safekeeping is done by the depositories NSDL or CDSL. SEBI is the regulator (not a custodian) and your broker is only a gateway — your shares are held in your name at the depository, which protects you if the broker fails.
quiz.approach: Because your shares sit at the depository in your name, verify holdings directly via CDSL/NSDL statements, not just the broker app.

lessons.title: Price Is Not Value: Market Cap and the P/E Ratio
lessons.concept:
Beginners obsess over share price — "₹50 feels cheap, ₹5,000 feels expensive." This is the single biggest rookie error. A share price is just the total value of a company sliced into a chosen number of pieces. The number that actually matters is market capitalisation = share price × total number of outstanding shares. MRF, India's costliest stock, trades above ₹1,00,000 a share — yet the whole company is worth far less than a giant like Reliance, whose shares cost a small fraction of that but which has vastly more of them. Comparing two companies by share price alone is like comparing two pizzas by the size of one slice without asking how many slices each was cut into.

Market cap also sorts the market into tiers you'll hear constantly. Under SEBI/AMFI rules, the largest 100 listed companies by market cap are large-caps (relatively stable — think TCS, HDFC Bank); companies ranked 101–250 are mid-caps; and everything from 251 onward is a small-cap (higher growth potential, higher risk). Your risk appetite should decide your mix — a first-timer piling into small-caps because they "look cheap" is taking on far more volatility than they realise.

To judge whether a price is expensive relative to earnings, use the P/E (price-to-earnings) ratio = share price ÷ earnings per share (EPS), where EPS = net profit ÷ number of shares. A P/E of 25 means you're paying ₹25 for every ₹1 of annual profit the company currently makes. A high P/E can mean the market expects fast growth — or that the stock is overhyped. P/E is only meaningful in comparison: against the company's own history, its sector peers, and the index. A profitable FMCG firm at P/E 60 and a bank at P/E 12 aren't simply "expensive vs cheap" until you know what's normal for each.
lessons.example: The market debates two stocks. Stock A trades at ₹80, Stock B at ₹4,000. A beginner buys A, thinking it's "50x cheaper." But A has 100 crore shares (market cap ₹8,000 cr) while B has just 1 crore shares (market cap ₹4,000 cr) — B is actually the smaller company. And if A earns ₹2 EPS (P/E 40) while B earns ₹400 EPS (P/E 10), B is far cheaper relative to its profits. The ₹80 "cheap" stock is the expensive one.
lessons.code:
Two stocks, same sector:

                      Stock A       Stock B
  Share price         ₹80           ₹4,000
  Shares outstanding  100 cr        1 cr
  Net profit          ₹200 cr       ₹400 cr

  Market cap = price x shares
    A = 80 x 100 cr    = ₹8,000 cr
    B = 4,000 x 1 cr   = ₹4,000 cr   -> B is the SMALLER company

  EPS = net profit / shares
    A = 200 / 100 = ₹2
    B = 400 / 1   = ₹400

  P/E = price / EPS
    A = 80 / 2     = 40   (expensive vs earnings)
    B = 4000 / 400 = 10   (cheap vs earnings)

A lower share price does NOT mean a cheaper company.
lessons.tryit: Pick any two companies in the same sector (say two banks, or two IT firms) on screener.in or your broker app. Write down each one's share price, market cap and P/E. Rank them by share price, then by market cap, then by P/E — and notice how the three rankings disagree.

mistakes.m: A ₹50 stock is cheaper than a ₹2,000 stock, so I get more value for my money.
mistakes.fix: Share price alone means nothing without the number of shares. Compare market cap (total worth) and P/E (price per rupee of profit), never the sticker price.

mistakes.m: A low P/E always means a bargain.
mistakes.fix: A low P/E can signal a genuine bargain — or a struggling business the market has given up on (a 'value trap'). Always ask why it's low and compare with sector peers.

mistakes.m: Small-cap stocks are safer because they cost less per share.
mistakes.fix: Per-share price says nothing about safety. Small-caps are the most volatile tier; their low prices reflect smaller, riskier businesses, not a discount.

links.t: AMFI — Association of Mutual Funds in India

quiz.q: Company X trades at ₹100 with 50 crore shares; Company Y trades at ₹2,000 with 1 crore shares. Which is worth more?
opts: Company Y, because its share price is higher
Company X, with a market cap of ₹5,000 crore vs Y's ₹2,000 crore
They are equal in value
Impossible to tell without the P/E
quiz.why: Market cap = price × shares. X = 100 × 50 cr = ₹5,000 cr; Y = 2,000 × 1 cr = ₹2,000 cr. Despite Y's far higher share price, X is the bigger company. Share price alone is a trap; the share count completes the picture.
quiz.approach: Always convert a share price into market cap before judging a company's size.

quiz.q: A stock has a P/E of 30. What does that most directly tell you?
opts: The company will grow 30% per year
You are paying ₹30 for every ₹1 of the company's current annual earnings
The stock will rise 30% this year
The company has 30 crore shares
quiz.why: P/E = price ÷ EPS, so a P/E of 30 means the market price is 30 times the company's per-share annual profit — you pay ₹30 today for ₹1 of current yearly earnings. It is a valuation multiple, not a promise of future growth or returns.
quiz.approach: Read P/E relative to the company's own history and its sector; a number in isolation tells you little.

quiz.q: Under SEBI/AMFI classification, a company ranked 60th by market capitalisation is a:
opts: Small-cap
Mid-cap
Large-cap
Micro-cap
quiz.why: The top 100 companies by market cap are large-caps, ranks 101–250 are mid-caps, and 251 onward are small-caps. Rank 60 sits comfortably inside the top 100, making it a large-cap — typically among the more stable, heavily-traded names.
quiz.approach: Use the official rank-based tiers to gauge a stock's risk profile, rather than guessing from its share price.

lessons.title: Reading a Company: Financials, Fundamental vs Technical
lessons.concept:
A share is a slice of a real business, so learn to read the business. Three numbers on any quarterly results statement carry most of the beginner signal. Revenue (the "top line" or sales) is the total money the company brought in. Net profit (PAT, profit after tax — the "bottom line") is what's left after every cost, interest payment and tax. EPS (earnings per share) = net profit ÷ number of shares, the profit attributable to each share you own. When a company "beats estimates," it usually means revenue and profit came in above what analysts expected — and the stock often jumps on the surprise, not the absolute number.

Two more checks keep beginners out of trouble. ROE (return on equity) = net profit ÷ shareholders' equity tells you how efficiently the company turns owners' money into profit — a consistently high ROE (say 15%+) signals a quality business. The debt-to-equity ratio shows how much the company borrows versus its own capital; a heavily indebted firm is fragile when interest rates rise or sales dip. A company can post rising profits while quietly drowning in debt, so never read profit alone.

There are two broad ways people analyse stocks. Fundamental analysis studies the business itself — revenue, profit, debt, management, competitive moat — to estimate what the company is worth, and suits long-term investors. Technical analysis ignores the business and studies price-and-volume charts to guess short-term direction, and suits traders. Neither is magic. For someone building wealth over years, fundamentals should anchor your decisions; charts, at most, help fine-tune timing. Treating a five-minute candlestick pattern as a reason to invest your savings is how beginners get hurt.
lessons.example: Two IT companies each report ₹500 crore of quarterly profit. Company P did it on ₹2,000 crore of equity (ROE 25%) with almost no debt; Company Q needed ₹5,000 crore of equity (ROE 10%) and heavy borrowing. Same headline profit, very different quality — P squeezes far more profit from each rupee of owners' capital and isn't at the mercy of lenders. A beginner reading only "₹500 cr profit" would miss the gap entirely.
lessons.code:
Same quarterly profit, different quality:

                        Company P     Company Q
  Net profit (PAT)      ₹500 cr       ₹500 cr
  Shareholders' equity  ₹2,000 cr     ₹5,000 cr
  Total debt            ₹100 cr       ₹4,000 cr

  ROE = PAT / equity
    P = 500 / 2000 = 25%    (excellent)
    Q = 500 / 5000 = 10%    (mediocre)

  Debt-to-equity = debt / equity
    P = 100 / 2000  = 0.05  (very safe)
    Q = 4000 / 5000 = 0.80  (leveraged, fragile)

Identical 'profit' headline, very different businesses.
lessons.tryit: On screener.in, open a company whose products you actually use (say HDFC Bank, Asian Paints or Tata Motors). Find three numbers from its latest results: quarterly revenue, net profit, and EPS. Then find its ROE and debt-to-equity. Write one sentence on whether it looks like a high-quality, low-debt business.

mistakes.m: Rising profit always means a healthy company worth buying.
mistakes.fix: Profit can rise while debt balloons or profitability (ROE) falls. Read profit alongside ROE and debt-to-equity to judge real quality.

mistakes.m: Technical and fundamental analysis are rival systems and I must pick one.
mistakes.fix: They answer different questions — fundamentals ask 'what is it worth?', technicals ask 'where might price go short-term?'. Long-term investors lead with fundamentals and may use charts only for timing.

mistakes.m: A company with high revenue must be profitable.
mistakes.fix: Revenue is money coming in; profit is what's left after costs. Many high-revenue firms run at a loss. Always travel from the top line down to the bottom line.

quiz.q: Net profit divided by the number of shares gives you:
opts: Market capitalisation
Earnings per share (EPS)
Return on equity (ROE)
The P/E ratio
quiz.why: EPS = net profit ÷ number of shares — it distributes the company's total profit across each share. Market cap uses share price, ROE divides profit by equity, and P/E divides price by EPS; only EPS is profit per share.
quiz.approach: Compute EPS first — it's the bridge between a company's total profit and per-share valuation measures like P/E.

quiz.q: Companies P and Q both earn ₹500 crore profit, but P's ROE is 25% and Q's is 10%. What does this indicate?
opts: Q is the better business because it is larger
P generates more profit per rupee of shareholders' capital, signalling higher quality
They are equally good since profit is identical
ROE is irrelevant when profit is the same
quiz.why: ROE measures how efficiently a company converts owners' capital into profit. Earning the same ₹500 cr on far less equity, P achieves 25% vs Q's 10% — it works its capital harder, a hallmark of a stronger business. Identical headline profit hides this gap.
quiz.approach: Compare profitability ratios like ROE, not just absolute profit, to judge business quality.

quiz.q: For a long-term investor building wealth over years, which approach should primarily anchor stock-picking?
opts: Five-minute candlestick chart patterns
Grey-market rumours
Fundamental analysis of the business's earnings, debt and quality
Whichever stock has the lowest share price
quiz.why: Long-term wealth tracks business performance, so fundamentals — earnings, debt, ROE, durability — are the right anchor. Technical (chart) analysis targets short-term price moves and, at most, helps time entries; it is not a basis for long-horizon investing.
quiz.approach: Let fundamentals drive what to buy for the long run; use technicals, if at all, only to fine-tune timing.

lessons.title: Reading the Whole Market: Indices, Flows, IPOs and Your Own Brain
lessons.concept:
You can't watch the thousands of listed companies, so the market uses indices as barometers. The Sensex is a basket of 30 large, financially-sound BSE companies; the Nifty 50 tracks 50 large NSE companies across sectors. Both are free-float market-cap weighted — bigger companies move the index more, and only freely tradable shares count (locked-in promoter holdings are excluded). When the news says "Sensex up 500 points," it means this basket rose, a rough proxy for large-cap sentiment. Companies are also grouped into sectors — IT, banking/financials, FMCG, auto, pharma — and money rotates between them: when the rupee weakens, IT exporters often gain; when interest rates fall, rate-sensitive banks and autos may rally.

Who moves this money in bulk? Two forces you'll see quoted daily: FIIs/FPIs (Foreign Institutional/Portfolio Investors — overseas funds) and DIIs (Domestic Institutional Investors — Indian mutual funds, insurers like LIC, and banks). The exchanges publish their net buying and selling every single day. For years, heavy FII selling could sink the market; increasingly, steady DII buying — fuelled by monthly SIP inflows from ordinary Indians — cushions those falls. These flows explain a lot of day-to-day movement, but chasing them blindly is a mistake; for a beginner they are context, not a trading signal.

Two final traps. IPOs (Initial Public Offerings) let a company sell shares to the public for the first time; they arrive wrapped in hype and a so-called GMP (Grey Market Premium) — an unofficial, unregulated, often-manipulated price floating around before listing that supposedly predicts listing gains. GMP is not data; it's a rumour with a number attached, and SEBI does not recognise it. The deepest trap of all is your own brain: herd mentality (buying because everyone is), FOMO, anchoring (fixating on the first price you saw), loss aversion (holding losers too long, selling winners too early), and recency bias (assuming the last few months predict the future). Markets punish emotion; a written plan and patience beat every hot tip.
lessons.example: A buzzy IPO shows a GMP of "+₹300" a week before listing, and WhatsApp groups scream it will "double on day one." A beginner applies with borrowed money expecting a sure ₹300 pop. On listing day sentiment cools, the stock opens flat and then slips below its issue price. The GMP was never an official figure — just grey-market chatter that evaporated. The disciplined investor who instead read the company's financials and valuation made a calm, unemotional call.
lessons.code:
Reading the daily institutional flows (illustrative figures):

  FII net = FII buy - FII sell
    = ₹12,000 cr bought - ₹15,000 cr sold = -₹3,000 cr (net SELLERS)

  DII net = DII buy - DII sell
    = ₹14,500 cr bought - ₹11,000 cr sold = +₹3,500 cr (net BUYERS)

  Net institutional flow = -3,000 + 3,500 = +₹500 cr

DIIs absorbed the FII selling and then some -> explains why the
index barely fell even as foreigners exited. Context, not a signal.
lessons.tryit: For the next five trading days, jot down the daily FII and DII net figures (published on the NSE/BSE sites and most finance portals) and whether the Nifty rose or fell. See for yourself whether DII buying is offsetting FII selling — and deliberately resist the urge to trade on it.

mistakes.m: A high GMP guarantees a strong listing, so I should apply to every hyped IPO.
mistakes.fix: GMP is an unofficial, unregulated, easily-manipulated grey-market rumour that SEBI doesn't recognise. Judge an IPO on its financials, valuation and prospectus (the RHP), never on GMP.

mistakes.m: If foreign investors (FIIs) are selling, the market must crash, so I should sell too.
mistakes.fix: FII selling is often absorbed by strong DII buying from domestic SIPs. Flows are context, not a personal trigger — reacting emotionally to them is itself a behavioural trap.

mistakes.m: The Sensex is up, so every stock and my whole portfolio must be up too.
mistakes.fix: Indices are weighted baskets of a few large companies; a handful of heavyweights can lift the index while most stocks — and your mid/small-caps — fall. Never read the index as 'the whole market.'

links.t: SEBI — regulator, IPO rules & investor protection

quiz.q: What does the 'GMP' (Grey Market Premium) around an IPO actually represent?
opts: An official price guaranteed by SEBI and the exchange
The company's audited fair value
An unofficial, unregulated grey-market rumour price with no guarantee
The final listing price set by the stock exchange
quiz.why: GMP is an informal price floating in an unofficial grey market before listing; it is neither regulated nor recognised by SEBI, is easily manipulated, and frequently fails to predict the actual listing. It's a rumour with a number attached, not official data.
quiz.approach: Evaluate IPOs from the prospectus (RHP), financials and valuation — never from GMP or WhatsApp hype.

quiz.q: The Nifty 50 and Sensex are weighted by:
opts: Equal weight — every company counts the same
Share price — the costliest stock dominates
Free-float market capitalisation — bigger, freely-tradable companies move it more
Alphabetical order of company names
quiz.why: Both indices use free-float market-cap weighting, so larger companies (by tradable market value) have more influence on index moves, and locked-in promoter shares are excluded. A high share price alone grants no weight — total free-float value does.
quiz.approach: Remember that a few heavyweight stocks can drive the index, so the index isn't a verdict on every stock you own.

quiz.q: On a day when FIIs are net sellers of ₹3,000 crore but DIIs are net buyers of ₹3,500 crore, the most reasonable reading is:
opts: The market is guaranteed to crash because foreigners are leaving
Domestic institutions are absorbing the foreign selling, cushioning the market — useful context, not a trade signal
You should immediately sell all your holdings
FII and DII figures have no relationship to market moves
quiz.why: DII net buying of ₹3,500 cr more than offsets the ₹3,000 cr of FII selling, so domestic money is absorbing the exit — which is why the index often holds up on such days. It explains price behaviour but, for a beginner, is background context rather than a reason to trade.
quiz.approach: Use FII/DII flows to understand market context, not as an emotional buy or sell trigger.

lessons.title: Market Cap vs Price: Why a ₹50 Stock Can Be 'Costlier' Than a ₹5,000 One
lessons.concept:
Open any trading app and the first number you see is the share price — and it quietly misleads you. A share priced at ₹50 is not 'cheaper' than one priced at ₹5,000. Price is just the company's total value chopped into a chosen number of slices. The number that actually measures a company's size is market capitalisation: market cap = share price × total shares outstanding. Change how many slices you cut the pie into and the price per slice changes, but the pie is exactly the same size.

SEBI's categorisation — used by every mutual fund — ranks all listed companies by market cap: the top 100 are large-caps, ranks 101 to 250 are mid-caps, and 251 onwards are small-caps. Large-caps like Reliance Industries (India's most valuable listed company, worth over ₹20 lakh crore) are widely tracked, liquid and relatively stable. Small-caps can double or halve far faster, because a little buying or selling moves a thinly-traded stock a lot.

So when someone says a stock is 'costly', ask: costly in price, or costly in value? Those are different questions. MRF trades above ₹1,00,000 a share yet its market cap is a fraction of many stocks priced under ₹1,000 — simply because MRF never split its shares into more pieces. Bonus issues and stock splits multiply the slices and shrink the price proportionally; they create zero wealth. Price tells you the size of one slice. Market cap tells you the size of the company. Valuation — the next lesson — tells you whether either is a good deal.
lessons.example: Reliance at roughly ₹1,400 a share with about 1,350 crore shares is worth around ₹19 lakh crore. MRF at roughly ₹1,40,000 a share with only about 42 lakh shares is worth around ₹60,000 crore. The ₹1.4 lakh 'expensive' stock is a far smaller company than the ₹1,400 'cheap' one — because it has vastly fewer slices.
lessons.code:
Market cap = price × shares outstanding

Company A: ₹50    × 200,00,00,000 shares = ₹10,000 crore
Company B: ₹5,000 ×   1,00,00,000 shares = ₹5,000 crore

Despite a 100x lower price, Company A is TWICE as big.

After a 1:1 stock split, Company B becomes:
  ₹2,500 × 2,00,00,000 shares = ₹5,000 crore  (unchanged — no wealth created)
lessons.tryit: On nseindia.com, look up MRF and Tata Motors. Note each one's share price and market cap, then divide market cap by price to back out the number of shares. Notice how the 'expensive' MRF is actually the smaller company.

mistakes.m: 'A ₹20 stock is cheaper than a ₹2,000 stock, so it has more room to grow.'
mistakes.fix: Price alone is meaningless — it depends on how many shares the company issued. Compare market cap for size and valuation (P/E) for whether it's a good deal.

mistakes.m: Thinking a stock split or bonus issue makes a share a better bargain.
mistakes.fix: A split/bonus multiplies the share count and cuts the price in proportion. Market cap is unchanged and no value is created — you own more slices of the same pie.

mistakes.m: Assuming a higher-priced share signals a better company.
mistakes.fix: There is no link between share price and company quality. A ₹1.4 lakh MRF is smaller than a ₹1,400 Reliance. Judge the business, market cap and valuation.

quiz.q: Company A trades at ₹50 with 200 crore shares; Company B trades at ₹5,000 with 1 crore shares. Which is bigger by market cap?
opts: Company A (₹10,000 crore)
Company B (₹5,000 crore)
They are equal
Impossible to say without more data
quiz.why: Market cap = price × shares. A = ₹50 × 200 crore = ₹10,000 crore; B = ₹5,000 × 1 crore = ₹5,000 crore. Despite a far lower share price, A is twice as large — because it has far more shares.
quiz.approach: Always multiply price by share count. Never judge company size from the share price alone.

quiz.q: A company announces a 1:1 stock split. What happens?
opts: Its market cap doubles
The price roughly halves and market cap stays the same
Existing holders instantly become wealthier
Its P/E ratio doubles
quiz.why: A split doubles the number of shares and halves the price per share, leaving market cap (and each holder's total value) unchanged. It just re-slices the same pie into more pieces.
quiz.approach: Treat splits and bonuses as cosmetic. Look at market cap and business fundamentals, not the lower sticker price.

quiz.q: Under SEBI's market-cap categorisation used by mutual funds, a 'large-cap' company is one that is:
opts: Priced above ₹1,000 per share
Ranked in the top 100 companies by market cap
Any company in the Nifty 50
Ranked in the top 500 companies by market cap
quiz.why: SEBI defines large-caps as the 1st–100th companies by full market capitalisation, mid-caps as 101st–250th, and small-caps as 251st onwards. Nifty 50 names are all large-caps, but the definition itself is the top 100 — not the index.
quiz.approach: Learn the top-100 / 101–250 / 251+ tiers; they drive how mutual funds are classified and how risky a fund is.

lessons.title: The P/E Ratio: How to Tell If You're Overpaying for Earnings
lessons.concept:
The price-to-earnings ratio turns the vague question 'is this expensive?' into a number you can compare. First, earnings per share (EPS) = net profit ÷ number of shares — the profit sitting behind each single share. Then P/E = share price ÷ EPS. A P/E of 25 means you are paying ₹25 today for every ₹1 the company earns in a year; flipped around, if earnings never grew it would take about 25 years of profit to earn your money back. Because it is a ratio, it lets you compare a ₹200 stock and a ₹4,000 stock on completely equal footing.

But a high P/E is not automatically 'overpriced', and a low P/E is not automatically a bargain — P/E bakes in expectations. A steady FMCG name like Hindustan Unilever often trades at 50–60 times earnings because investors expect reliable growth for decades; a PSU bank might trade at 6–8 times because its growth is lumpy and uncertain. The Nifty 50 as a whole has historically hovered around 20–24 times. The rule: compare P/E within the same sector and against a company's own history — never a bank against an FMCG.

P/E also has blind spots. A loss-making company has negative or nil EPS, so its P/E is meaningless (which is why you can't value an early-stage startup this way). One-off gains — selling land, a tax write-back — can flatter earnings and fake a low P/E. Cyclical businesses like steel or cement look 'cheapest' at the very top of their cycle, when profits peak and are about to fall. So use P/E as your first question, then cross-check with price-to-book (P/B), return on equity (ROE), debt and cash flow — the tools of the next lesson.
lessons.example: Suppose Infosys earns ₹65 EPS and trades at ₹1,600, giving a P/E of about 25. A PSU bank earns ₹40 EPS and trades at ₹320, a P/E of 8. The bank looks 'cheaper', but that gap mostly reflects Infosys's steadier, higher-quality, more predictable earnings — not a mispricing you can simply pocket.
lessons.code:
Net profit  = ₹500 crore
Shares      = 25 crore
EPS         = 500 / 25 = ₹20 per share

Share price = ₹600
P/E         = 600 / 20 = 30x
  → you pay ₹30 for every ₹1 of annual profit.

Earnings yield = 1 / P/E = 1 / 30 ≈ 3.3% per year
  → a quick sanity check against, say, an FD or bond yield.
lessons.tryit: Pick two companies in the same sector (e.g., TCS and Infosys), note each one's P/E from screener.in or the NSE site, and write one sentence explaining what the market must be expecting from the higher-P/E one.

mistakes.m: 'Low P/E always means the stock is cheap and a bargain.'
mistakes.fix: A low P/E can be a value trap — falling earnings, a dying business, or a cyclical at its profit peak. Ask WHY it's low before assuming it's a deal.

mistakes.m: 'High P/E always means the stock is overvalued.'
mistakes.fix: Fast, durable growth can justify a high P/E. What matters is the P/E relative to the company's growth, its sector, and its own history.

mistakes.m: Comparing P/E across different sectors (e.g., an FMCG at 55 vs a bank at 8).
mistakes.fix: Each sector has its own normal range because of different growth and risk. Compare like with like — bank vs bank, IT vs IT.

quiz.q: A company has a net profit of ₹500 crore, 25 crore shares, and a share price of ₹600. What is its P/E?
quiz.why: EPS = 500 ÷ 25 = ₹20. P/E = price ÷ EPS = 600 ÷ 20 = 30. You are paying ₹30 for every ₹1 of annual earnings.
quiz.approach: Always compute EPS first (profit ÷ shares), then divide price by EPS. Don't confuse profit with EPS.

quiz.q: Stock A (a PSU bank) has a P/E of 8; Stock B (an FMCG) has a P/E of 55. Which is the better buy?
opts: A — a lower P/E is always cheaper and better
B — a higher P/E signals a better company
Neither is clear from P/E alone; compare within sector and against growth
Whichever has the lower share price
quiz.why: P/E must be judged against a company's sector norms, growth prospects and its own history. A bank and an FMCG have very different normal ranges, so the two numbers aren't directly comparable and don't, by themselves, name a winner.
quiz.approach: Use P/E as a starting question, not a verdict. Compare like-with-like and cross-check with ROE, debt and cash flow.

quiz.q: Why can't you compute a meaningful trailing P/E for a loss-making startup?
opts: Because its EPS is negative or nil, making the ratio meaningless
Because SEBI prohibits it
Because its share price is unknown
Because it has no shares outstanding
quiz.why: P/E = price ÷ EPS. If the company lost money, EPS is negative or zero, so the ratio is either negative or undefined — it tells you nothing. Loss-making firms are valued using other tools (e.g., price-to-sales).
quiz.approach: When earnings are negative, drop P/E and switch to revenue-, cash-flow- or book-value-based measures.

lessons.title: FIIs, DIIs, and the Daily Tug-of-War That Moves the Nifty
lessons.concept:
An index is a thermometer for the whole market. The BSE Sensex tracks 30 large companies; the NSE Nifty 50 tracks 50. Both are free-float market-capitalisation weighted — the bigger a company is (counting only the shares actually available to trade), the more it pushes the index, so Reliance or HDFC Bank moves the Nifty far more than its smallest member. Beyond the headline indices sit sectoral ones — Bank Nifty, Nifty IT, Nifty Pharma — which reveal whether a move is broad-based or driven by a single sector.

Two big institutional forces tug at the market every day. FIIs (foreign portfolio investors) run huge pools of global money and can pour in or pull out fast, chasing global interest rates and the rupee — which is why they're nicknamed 'hot money'. DIIs — domestic mutual funds, insurers like LIC, and pension funds — are the home team. The quiet hero here is the SIP: crores of Indians auto-investing every month means DIIs receive a steady river of cash (monthly SIP inflows have grown past ₹25,000 crore) that they deploy regardless of the mood. NSE and BSE publish provisional FII/DII buy-sell figures after every session.

This tug-of-war explains a lot. Through 2022, FIIs were heavy net sellers as US rates spiked, yet Indian markets fell far less than feared because SIP-fed DIIs kept buying the dip. A 'bull market' is a sustained rise powered by optimism; a 'bear market' is a sustained fall, often defined as roughly 20% or more off the peak. Watching flows is useful context — but FIIs aren't always right, and blindly mirroring hot money is a reliable way to buy high and sell low.
lessons.example: In 2021–22, as the US Fed began hiking rates, FIIs withdrew tens of thousands of crores from Indian equities. In an earlier era that would have sunk the market — but relentless SIP money flowing through domestic mutual funds absorbed much of the selling, and the Nifty's fall was far shallower than in previous FII exoduses such as 2008.
lessons.code:
One trading session (provisional, ₹ crore):
  FII net = SELL 3,000   (net seller)
  DII net = BUY  3,400   (net buyer)
  Net institutional = -3,000 + 3,400 = +400
    → domestic buying more than absorbed foreign selling.

A single day tells you little — watch the trend over weeks.
lessons.tryit: On nseindia.com, find the daily 'FII/DII activity' figures. Track net FII and net DII for five consecutive sessions and note who was the bigger force each day.

mistakes.m: 'FIIs are selling, so I should sell too.'
mistakes.fix: FIIs react to global factors, not necessarily to Indian fundamentals, and DIIs often absorb their selling. Treat flows as one input among many, not a signal to copy.

mistakes.m: Reading the index level (e.g., 'Sensex at 80,000') as a sign stocks are cheap or expensive.
mistakes.fix: The level is just points measured from a historical base. To judge valuation, look at the index's P/E, not its raw number.

mistakes.m: Assuming Sensex and Nifty are the same thing.
mistakes.fix: Sensex is 30 stocks on the BSE; Nifty 50 is 50 stocks on the NSE. They overlap heavily but are computed separately and can differ.

quiz.q: The Nifty 50 index is weighted by:
opts: Equal weight for all 50 stocks
Each company's share price
Free-float market capitalisation
Each company's dividend yield
quiz.why: Both the Nifty 50 and the Sensex use free-float market-cap weighting, so larger companies (by tradable shares) move the index more. A high-priced but small company barely nudges it.
quiz.approach: Remember that a few heavyweights drive index moves. A 'market up' day may really be two or three giants moving.

quiz.q: In 2022, FIIs were heavy net sellers, yet Indian markets held up better than feared. The main domestic cushion was:
opts: The RBI buying stocks directly
Steady DII buying fed by monthly SIP inflows
A government ban on selling shares
Foreign banks stepping in to buy
quiz.why: Domestic institutions — funded by relentless monthly SIP inflows into mutual funds — kept buying as FIIs sold, absorbing much of the pressure. The RBI does not buy equities, and there was no selling ban.
quiz.approach: Watch FII and DII flows together; domestic SIP money has become a powerful, steady counterweight to volatile foreign flows.

quiz.q: A 'bear market' most accurately describes:
opts: A single red trading day
A sustained decline, often around 20% or more from the peak
Any day on which FIIs are net sellers
A day the Sensex hits a new all-time high
quiz.why: A bear market is a prolonged, broad decline — commonly quoted as roughly a 20%+ drop from the recent peak — not one bad session. A bull market is the sustained opposite.
quiz.approach: Distinguish daily noise from a genuine trend. One red day is not a bear market; a sustained drawdown is.

lessons.title: IPOs, Grey Market Premium, and Your Own Worst Enemy
lessons.concept:
An IPO (initial public offering) is a company selling shares to the public for the first time. Before it can, it files a DRHP (draft red herring prospectus) with SEBI — a detailed document disclosing its finances, risks and exactly how it will use the money raised. It sets a price band and a lot size; retail investors apply through ASBA/UPI, where the money is only blocked in your bank account until shares are allotted (and unblocked if you get none). Large 'anchor investors' come in a day before the issue opens. Read at least the 'Risk Factors' and 'Objects of the Issue' in the DRHP before applying — that is the company telling you, in writing, what could go wrong.

Now the trap: Grey Market Premium (GMP). This is an unofficial, off-the-books price at which an IPO's shares 'trade' before listing, quoted by grey-market dealers. It is completely unregulated, not recognised by SEBI, driven by rumour and sentiment, and easily manipulated to lure applicants. A high GMP feels like a guaranteed listing pop — but it predicts nothing about the real listing, let alone the long term. Paytm listed in 2021 amid enormous hype and fell about 27% on debut; the giant LIC IPO in 2022 listed below its ₹949 issue price. GMP is noise dressed up as a tip.

Your biggest opponent, though, is your own brain. FOMO and herding push you to buy what has already run up; anchoring makes you refuse to sell a loser until it 'gets back to my price' — a price the market has never heard of. Loss aversion makes a ₹10,000 loss sting more than a ₹10,000 gain feels good, so you sell winners early and cling to losers (the 'disposition effect'). Recency bias tricks you into assuming today's trend lasts forever. The antidotes are boring and they work: a written plan, diversification, sensible position sizing, and tuning out the daily noise — GMP included.
lessons.example: Paytm's 2021 IPO was India's largest at the time (about ₹18,300 crore) and dominated the headlines. Grey-market chatter implied strong listing gains; instead the stock opened below its ₹2,150 issue price and closed roughly 27% down on its very first day, taking years to recover. The 'sure thing' the crowd was chasing turned out to be exactly the thing to be wary of.
lessons.code:
IPO price band: ₹100–105, lot size: 140 shares

Apply at cut-off (₹105): 140 × 105 = ₹14,700 blocked via UPI
  → if NOT allotted, the ₹14,700 is simply unblocked — never spent.

GMP quoted at '+₹40'?
  → an unregulated, unverifiable rumour — not a promise, not SEBI-recognised.
lessons.tryit: Find a recent or upcoming IPO on the SEBI or NSE website, open its DRHP/RHP, and read only the 'Risk Factors' and 'Objects of the Issue' sections. Write down the two risks that would most affect the business.

mistakes.m: 'A high GMP means a guaranteed listing gain.'
mistakes.fix: GMP is an unregulated grey-market rumour that is easily manipulated and doesn't predict listing performance. Ignore it and evaluate the business and valuation from the DRHP.

mistakes.m: Treating IPOs as a quick way to double your money.
mistakes.fix: Many IPOs list flat or below their issue price (e.g., LIC, Paytm). Apply because you want to own the business at that price, not because of hype.

mistakes.m: 'I'll sell once it climbs back to my buy price.'
mistakes.fix: This is anchoring — the market has no memory of your entry price. Decide to hold or sell based on the company's fundamentals and valuation today, not your cost.

quiz.q: Grey Market Premium (GMP) is best described as:
opts: SEBI's official estimate of the listing price
An unofficial, unregulated pre-listing premium with no legal standing
A guaranteed listing-day gain
The upper end of the IPO price band
quiz.why: GMP is quoted by grey-market dealers before listing, is not regulated or recognised by SEBI, and is driven by rumour and easily manipulated. It carries no guarantee and often fails to predict the actual listing.
quiz.approach: Never let GMP drive an IPO decision. Base it on the DRHP — the business, valuation and risks.

quiz.q: The 'disposition effect' describes investors who tend to:
opts: Sell winners too early and hold losers too long
Always manage to buy at the exact bottom
Diversify their portfolios perfectly
Rely only on company fundamentals
quiz.why: Driven by loss aversion, investors book small gains quickly (to 'lock in' a win) but refuse to realise losses, hoping losers recover — the opposite of disciplined selling. This systematically hurts returns.
quiz.approach: Judge each holding on its forward prospects, not on whether you're currently up or down on it.

quiz.q: Which high-profile 2021 IPO (issue price ₹2,150) fell about 27% on its debut day despite enormous hype?
opts: Infosys
Paytm (One97 Communications)
TCS
Reliance Industries
quiz.why: Paytm's parent One97 Communications ran India's largest IPO at the time, yet the stock listed below its ₹2,150 issue price and closed roughly 27% down on day one — a textbook case of hype not translating into listing gains.
quiz.approach: Big brand and big buzz do not guarantee big listing gains. Separate the story from the price you're paying.

lessons.title: Why a Low P/E Isn't 'Cheap': Valuation Past the Headline Multiple
lessons.concept:
A P/E ratio is simply the price you pay today for ₹1 of a company's annual earnings. Flip it over and you get the earnings yield (1 ÷ P/E): a stock at 25x earnings has a 4% earnings yield, which you can hold up against the ~7% you'd earn risk-free on a government bond. But the multiple itself is never a price tag — it is a compressed claim about three things: how durable the growth is, how good the returns on capital are, and how risky/predictable the business is. A high multiple is the market saying 'I'll pay up because this compounds for years'; a low one says 'I don't trust these earnings to last.'

This is why a low P/E is one of the most dangerous illusions for a new investor. Cyclical businesses — steel, cement, autos, sugar — show their LOWEST P/E at the top of the cycle (when earnings are temporarily fat) and their HIGHEST P/E at the bottom (when earnings collapse). Buying the '6x' steel stock at peak profits is often buying at the worst moment. P/E also ignores debt entirely and is easily distorted by one-off gains. That's where two better tools come in: EV/EBITDA, which adds debt and subtracts cash so you compare firms on an apples-to-apples, capital-structure-neutral basis; and PEG (P/E ÷ growth rate), which tempers a scary-looking P/E against how fast the company is actually growing.

The mental model to lock in: a fair multiple rises with growth durability and return quality (ROCE), and falls with debt, cyclicality and capital intensity. So a 55x FMCG major and an 8x steel maker can BOTH be fairly priced — they are different animals. Never compare P/E blindly across sectors. For banks and NBFCs, don't even use EV/EBITDA (interest is their core business, so EBITDA is meaningless) — value them on Price-to-Book and ROE instead. Compare within a peer group and against the company's own history, not across the whole market.
lessons.example: On the same day you might see an FMCG major trading near 55x earnings and a steel producer near 8x. The steel name looks 'cheaper', but its earnings are near a commodity-cycle peak and can halve in a downturn — which pushes the P/E UP, not down — while the FMCG's earnings are steadier and grow every year. Neither multiple is right or wrong in isolation; each reflects growth durability, ROCE and cyclicality. (Live multiples move daily; the point is the logic, not the exact figures.)
lessons.code:
Price = ₹500, EPS (TTM) = ₹20
P/E = 500 / 20 = 25x
Earnings yield = 1 / 25 = 4.0%   (vs ~7% G-Sec)

Expected EPS growth = 25%/yr
PEG = 25 / 25 = 1.0   (roughly fair for the growth)

EV/EBITDA:
  Market cap            = ₹50,000 cr
  + Total debt          = ₹10,000 cr
  − Cash & equivalents  = ₹ 4,000 cr
  Enterprise Value (EV) = ₹56,000 cr
  EBITDA                = ₹ 8,000 cr
  EV/EBITDA = 56,000 / 8,000 = 7.0x
lessons.tryit: Pick any two companies from the SAME sector on Screener.in or the NSE site. Note each one's P/E, and then compute PEG (P/E ÷ expected growth) and, for the non-financials, EV/EBITDA. Write one sentence on why the 'lower P/E' one is or isn't actually cheaper.

mistakes.m: Assuming a low P/E automatically means the stock is undervalued and a good buy.
mistakes.fix: Check where the company sits in its earnings cycle, how much debt it carries, and its growth. Confirm with EV/EBITDA and PEG before calling anything 'cheap'.

mistakes.m: Comparing the P/E of a bank with a manufacturer, or comparing P/E across unrelated sectors.
mistakes.fix: Compare within a peer set. Value banks/NBFCs on Price-to-Book and ROE; EBITDA-based ratios don't apply to lenders.

mistakes.m: Using a headline trailing P/E that includes a one-time gain (asset sale, tax write-back).
mistakes.fix: Normalise earnings — strip out exceptional/one-off items and check forward estimates, not just the reported number.

quiz.q: A steel company trades at a P/E of 6 while an FMCG company trades at 55. What is the most sensible reading?
opts: The steel stock is clearly cheaper and therefore the better buy
P/E is the only ratio you need to compare the two
The steel company's low P/E may reflect peak-cycle earnings and cyclicality, not a bargain
The FMCG is obviously overpriced and should be sold
quiz.why: Cyclical businesses show their lowest P/E when earnings are temporarily at a peak; if profits then fall, the P/E rises rather than the stock being 'cheap'. Multiples embed growth, quality and risk, so you cannot compare across sectors on P/E alone.
quiz.approach: Judge a multiple within a peer group and across the earnings cycle, and cross-check with EV/EBITDA and PEG.

quiz.q: Why is EV/EBITDA often preferred over P/E for comparing capital-intensive companies?
opts: It always produces a smaller, more attractive number
It is neutral to capital structure and ignores differences in depreciation and tax, aiding fair comparison
It is the best ratio specifically for valuing banks
It includes the company's dividend payout
quiz.why: Enterprise Value adds debt and subtracts cash, and EBITDA is measured before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation. So two firms with very different leverage or depreciation policies become comparable. Banks are the key exception, since interest is their core revenue.
quiz.approach: Use EV/EBITDA for leveraged, capital-heavy non-financials; use Price-to-Book and ROE for banks and NBFCs.

quiz.q: A stock has a PEG ratio of about 1.0. What does this roughly indicate?
opts: The share price is guaranteed to rise
The company carries no debt
The dividend yield equals the growth rate
The P/E is roughly in line with the earnings growth rate — a rough 'fairly priced for growth' signal
quiz.why: PEG = P/E ÷ growth rate (in %). A value near 1 means you're paying about one unit of multiple per unit of growth; below 1 hints at cheap-for-growth and above 1 at rich-for-growth. It's a heuristic, only as good as the growth estimate behind it.
quiz.approach: Use PEG to put a high P/E in the context of growth, but always sanity-check the growth assumption you plugged in.

lessons.title: Reading Financials Like an Analyst: Cash, Returns, and Red Flags
lessons.concept:
Every listed company gives you three linked statements, and knowing how they relate is what separates an analyst from a tip-follower. The Profit & Loss (P&L) shows accrual profit — an accountant's opinion about revenue and costs. The Balance Sheet is a snapshot of what the company owns and owes. And the Cash Flow Statement is the truth serum: it strips out accounting judgement and shows the actual cash that moved. The single most useful habit you can build is to check, over 3–5 years, whether Cash Flow from Operations (CFO) tracks reported net profit. When profits keep rising but CFO stays flat or negative while receivables and inventory balloon, that is the classic fingerprint of aggressive revenue recognition or channel-stuffing — 'profit' that lives on the balance sheet, not in the bank.

Next, return ratios, because a company can be profitable and still destroy value. ROE (Net Profit ÷ Shareholders' Equity) tells you the return on owners' money; ROCE (EBIT ÷ Capital Employed) tells you the pre-tax return on ALL capital, debt plus equity. The DuPont breakdown is the analyst's scalpel: ROE = Net Margin × Asset Turnover × Financial Leverage. It reveals WHERE a high ROE comes from — fat margins, efficient asset use, or simply piling on debt. A 25% ROE built on leverage is fragile; the same ROE built on margins and turnover is high quality. The ultimate test is ROCE versus cost of capital: a business consistently earning 25% ROCE against an ~11–12% cost of capital is compounding wealth, while one earning below its cost of capital is quietly destroying it even as it reports 'profits'.

Finally, keep a red-flag checklist and read the notes, not just the headline numbers. Watch the shareholding pattern for promoter share pledging (promoters borrowing against their own stock — a sign of stress). Watch for a rising volume of related-party transactions, auditor qualifications or a sudden auditor/CFO resignation, ballooning contingent liabilities, and repeated equity dilution. No single item proves fraud — but a cluster of them is a perfectly good reason to walk away and find a cleaner business.
lessons.example: Two firms both report ₹500 crore net profit. Firm A's operating cash flow is ₹520 crore and its receivables are steady — cash is following profit. Firm B shows just ₹90 crore of operating cash flow, receivables up 60%, and inventory piling up — the 'profit' is trapped in the balance sheet. On the P&L they look identical; the cash flow statement, plus a shareholding pattern showing (say) 40% of promoter shares pledged, tell you exactly which one to avoid.
lessons.code:
DuPont: ROE = Net margin × Asset turnover × Leverage

Net profit = ₹500 cr,  Revenue = ₹5,000 cr
Total assets = ₹4,000 cr,  Equity = ₹2,000 cr

Net margin     = 500 / 5,000  = 10%
Asset turnover = 5,000 / 4,000 = 1.25
Leverage (A/E) = 4,000 / 2,000 = 2.0

ROE = 10% × 1.25 × 2.0 = 25%
Check: 500 / 2,000 = 25%   OK

Same 25% ROE with Leverage = 1.0 would force
margin/turnover to do the work — a far safer 25%.
lessons.tryit: Open the Cash Flow Statement of any company you own or follow (Screener.in or the annual report). Line up Net Profit against Cash Flow from Operations for the last five years. If CFO is consistently below profit, dig into receivables and inventory and write down what you find.

mistakes.m: Judging a company on reported net profit or EPS alone.
mistakes.fix: Reconcile profit against Cash Flow from Operations over 3–5 years. Cash that never shows up is the loudest warning sign.

mistakes.m: Celebrating a high ROE without asking where it comes from.
mistakes.fix: Run the DuPont breakdown. ROE driven mainly by leverage is fragile — debt magnifies both returns and losses.

mistakes.m: Ignoring the shareholding pattern and audit notes because 'the numbers look great'.
mistakes.fix: Check promoter pledging, related-party transactions and any auditor/CFO changes. A cluster of red flags overrides a pretty P&L.

quiz.q: A company reports rising net profit for three straight years, but its operating cash flow stays flat-to-negative while receivables balloon. What is the most likely interpretation?
opts: It is clearly a great compounder
A red flag — profits may be inflated by aggressive revenue recognition, because cash isn't following earnings
Completely normal, nothing to investigate
It simply means the company pays high dividends
quiz.why: Over time, cash from operations should track net profit. A persistent gap alongside rising receivables suggests sales are being booked without cash actually being collected — the hallmark of aggressive accounting.
quiz.approach: Always reconcile reported profit with operating cash flow across several years before you trust the EPS.

quiz.q: A DuPont breakdown shows Company X's high ROE comes almost entirely from a large equity multiplier (leverage). What does this imply?
opts: The company clearly has very low debt
The ROE is high quality and safe
The ROE is leverage-driven and therefore more fragile — debt amplifies both gains and losses
The company's operating margins must be excellent
quiz.why: ROE = margin × asset turnover × leverage. When leverage is doing the heavy lifting, returns look great in good times but can collapse in a downturn, because debt magnifies losses just as it magnified profits.
quiz.approach: Prefer ROE driven by margins and asset efficiency; treat leverage-heavy ROE with caution.

quiz.q: Which is the single strongest test of whether a business is genuinely creating value?
opts: ROCE consistently above its cost of capital
A high reported net profit figure
A steadily rising share price
A low headline P/E ratio
quiz.why: Earning a return on capital that exceeds the cost of that capital is the very definition of value creation. A firm can report accounting profit while still earning less than its cost of capital — quietly destroying value.
quiz.approach: Compare ROCE to the company's approximate cost of capital over several years, not just this year's profit.

lessons.title: Plumbing and Flows: How Orders Fill and Who Really Moves the Market
lessons.concept:
When you tap 'Buy', you're choosing how your order meets the order book, and the choice matters. A market order fills immediately at the best available price — but in a thin stock it 'walks up the book', eating higher and higher asks, so you get speed at the cost of price certainty (slippage). A limit order fixes your price but not your fill: you may get nothing. A stop-loss (SL) order has a trigger price plus a limit; an SL-M (stop-loss market) has only a trigger and then becomes a market order once hit. Guarding all of this are circuit filters: individual stocks have daily price bands (commonly 5%, 10% or 20%), and market-wide circuit breakers on the Nifty/Sensex halt ALL trading at 10%, 15% and 20% moves, with a 20% swing stopping trading for the day. Surveillance frameworks like ASM and GSM flag unusually volatile or manipulated counters. And since January 2023, India settles trades on a T+1 basis — cash and shares change hands the next working day.

Now, the flows that actually push the tape. Two forces dominate the daily net buying and selling that exchanges publish: FPIs (Foreign Portfolio Investors) and DIIs (Domestic Institutional Investors — mutual funds and insurers like LIC). For years FPIs set the mood; today the DIIs have a structural firehose behind them — monthly SIP inflows into mutual funds now run well over ₹20,000 crore, money that gets invested every single month regardless of headlines. That steady domestic bid is why heavy foreign selling no longer automatically sinks the market. If you watch only FII numbers, you'll routinely be surprised by where prices actually go.

Finally, the hidden hands behind moves that seem to have 'no news'. When an index like the Nifty 50 is reconstituted, every passive/index fund tracking it MUST buy the newly added stock and sell the removed one near the effective date — pure mechanical flow that swings prices regardless of fundamentals. And around monthly derivatives expiry, large open interest in index and stock options can 'pin' prices, while futures rollovers tug the spot market. You never have to trade these forces, but recognising them explains a huge fraction of otherwise-baffling intraday moves.
lessons.example: Through calendar 2022, FPIs pulled over ₹1 lakh crore out of Indian equities as global interest rates spiked — a genuine wall of foreign selling. Yet the Nifty 50 ended the year roughly flat-to-slightly-positive, because domestic institutions — mutual funds and insurers, fed by steady SIP inflows of around ₹13,000 crore every month that year — absorbed almost everything the foreigners sold. Anyone watching only FII flows would have been badly misled on the market's direction.
lessons.code:
Order book (asks) for an illiquid stock:
  200 shares @ ₹101
  150 shares @ ₹104
  300 shares @ ₹109

MARKET BUY 500 shares fills against:
  200 @101 + 150 @104 + 150 @109
  = 20,200 + 15,600 + 16,350 = ₹52,150
  Avg fill = 52,150 / 500 = ₹104.3  (not ₹101!)

LIMIT BUY 500 @ ₹102:
  fills 200 @101, the rest rests unfilled
  -> price certain, quantity uncertain
lessons.tryit: For one week, note the daily net FII and net DII figures (published on the NSE site and most broker apps). On any day the two point in opposite directions, jot down what the Nifty actually did — you'll quickly see why watching only one side is misleading.

mistakes.m: Using market orders on illiquid or small-cap stocks to 'get in fast'.
mistakes.fix: Use limit orders there. Check the bid-ask spread and market depth first — a market order can walk up a thin book and fill far above the quoted price.

mistakes.m: Believing FIIs alone dictate market direction.
mistakes.fix: Track DII flows too. SIP-powered domestic buying now regularly counterbalances foreign selling; read both numbers together.

mistakes.m: Confusing SL with SL-M, or not using stops at all.
mistakes.fix: Understand that SL has a trigger AND a limit price, while SL-M triggers then fills at market. Know which one you've placed before you rely on it.

quiz.q: You want to buy an illiquid small-cap that has a wide bid-ask spread. Which order type is safest for price?
opts: A market order, for a guaranteed quick fill
A limit order set at your acceptable price, to avoid slippage
Always an SL-M order
The order type makes no difference here
quiz.why: In a thin book a market order walks up successively higher asks, so your average fill can be far above the top quote. A limit order caps the price you pay, at the cost of possibly not filling fully.
quiz.approach: Use limit orders in illiquid names and inspect depth and spread before placing them.

quiz.q: In 2022, FPIs were heavy net sellers of Indian equities, yet the Nifty barely fell. What was the main cushion?
opts: The RBI directly buying shares in the market
Domestic institutions, powered by steady monthly SIP inflows, buying the dip
Listed companies buying back all their shares
FPIs secretly buying the stock back
quiz.why: DIIs — mutual funds and insurers — are fed by consistent monthly SIP flows that get invested regardless of sentiment. In 2022 that domestic bid absorbed the bulk of foreign selling, keeping the index roughly flat.
quiz.approach: Watch both FII and DII daily flows; domestic flows now regularly offset foreign selling.

quiz.q: A stock jumps sharply right around a Nifty index reconstitution, with no company news. The most likely cause is:
opts: Illegal insider trading, always
Passive and index funds mechanically buying (on inclusion) or selling (on exclusion) to track the index
The company quietly changed its name
A market-wide circuit breaker was triggered
quiz.why: Funds that track an index are obliged to hold exactly its constituents. When a stock is added or removed, they must trade it near the effective date, moving the price purely on flow, not fundamentals.
quiz.approach: Expect mechanical price effects around index rebalancing and derivatives expiry, independent of news.

lessons.title: Don't Be Exit Liquidity: IPOs, GMP, and the Behavioural Traps
lessons.concept:
Before you apply to any IPO, split the issue into its two halves. A fresh issue means new shares are sold and the money flows INTO the company — for growth, capex or paying down debt. An Offer for Sale (OFS) means existing shareholders — promoters, private-equity funds, early backers — are selling THEIR shares, and every rupee goes to them, not the business. An IPO that is mostly OFS, with insiders cashing out near a market high, should prompt one blunt question: why are the people who know this business best selling now? Read the Red Herring Prospectus (RHP) for the use of proceeds, the valuation versus already-listed peers, and pending litigation. Note also the plumbing: anchor investors are allotted shares a day before the issue opens with a lock-in (50% for 30 days, 50% for 90 days), and the promoters' minimum 20% contribution is locked for 18 months — so a wave of selling can arrive precisely when those locks expire.

Now the number retail investors obsess over and should mostly ignore: the Grey Market Premium (GMP). It is an unofficial, unregulated quote from a handful of grey-market dealers about where shares are 'trading' before listing. It reflects short-term hype, is trivially easy to manipulate to lure applicants, and says nothing about the value of the business. Treat GMP as noise, not signal. A 2024 SEBI study found that investors sold roughly half their allotted IPO shares (by value) within a week of listing — most applicants are flippers chasing the pop, which means the enthusiastic retail buyer who piles in AT the listing high is often providing the exit liquidity for anchors and promoters. The base rate matters too: a large share of IPOs eventually trade below their issue price, so 'apply to everything for listing gains' is a losing strategy over time.

Underneath all of this sit the behavioural traps that empty wallets across every market. Anchoring — fixating on the 52-week high or on your own purchase price as if it were fair value. Recency bias — assuming the last rally continues forever. The disposition effect — booking small gains too early while stubbornly holding big losers, hoping to 'get back to breakeven'. Herding and FOMO, overconfidence, and confirmation bias (reading only what agrees with you). Prospect theory, from Kahneman and Tversky, shows that a loss hurts about twice as much as an equal gain feels good — which is exactly why people freeze on losing positions. The only reliable antidote is process: a written thesis for every buy, sensible position sizing, and exit rules pre-committed BEFORE you're emotionally invested.
lessons.example: A buzzy IPO trends on social media with a fat 'GMP', lists with a 60% pop, and retail investors pour in at the high — then it drifts below its issue price over the following months. A 2024 SEBI study found investors sold roughly half their allotted IPO shares by value within a week of listing: most applicants are flippers chasing the listing pop, and the late retail buyer frequently ends up as the exit liquidity for the anchor investors and promoters who were selling into that very pop.
lessons.tryit: Pick one recent IPO. Open its RHP (available on the SEBI site or the lead manager's page) and find two things: (1) the split between Fresh Issue and Offer for Sale, and (2) the 'objects of the issue' / use of proceeds. Write one line on how much money actually reaches the company versus the selling shareholders.

mistakes.m: Treating the Grey Market Premium as a signal of fair value or a reason to apply.
mistakes.fix: Ignore GMP for the invest/skip decision — it's unregulated, manipulable noise. Base your call on the RHP, the fresh-issue vs OFS split, and peer valuations.

mistakes.m: Applying to every IPO for quick listing gains.
mistakes.fix: Respect the base rate: many IPOs list flat or fall below issue price. Judge each on business merit and valuation, and size any speculative punt tiny.

mistakes.m: Holding a losing stock because it's 'below my buy price' and waiting to break even.
mistakes.fix: That's the disposition effect and anchoring to cost. Decide based on the forward-looking thesis, not your entry price, and pre-set exit rules.

links.t: SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India)

quiz.q: An IPO is almost entirely an Offer for Sale (OFS) by promoters and early investors. What does that tell you?
opts: The company will receive a large amount of growth capital
It guarantees strong listing gains
The Grey Market Premium is bound to be high
The proceeds go to the selling shareholders, not the company — insiders are cashing out, so ask why
quiz.why: In an OFS, no fresh money enters the business; the cash goes to the shareholders who are selling. That isn't automatically bad, but it changes the key question to: why are the best-informed owners exiting now?
quiz.approach: Always check the fresh-issue vs OFS split and the 'use of proceeds' in the RHP before applying.

quiz.q: How should you treat the Grey Market Premium (GMP) when deciding on an IPO?
opts: As an unofficial, unregulated, easily-manipulated sentiment number — essentially noise
As a reliable estimate of the company's fair value
As SEBI's official pre-listing price
As a guarantee of listing-day gains
quiz.why: GMP is quoted by a small set of unregulated grey-market dealers and can be pumped to attract applicants. It captures short-term hype, not business fundamentals, so it shouldn't drive the decision to invest.
quiz.approach: Ignore GMP for the invest/skip call; rely on the RHP and comparison with listed peer valuations.

quiz.q: The 'disposition effect' describes investors who tend to:
opts: Buy only high-dividend stocks
Sell winners too early while holding losers too long, anchoring to their purchase price
Always mirror whatever the FIIs are doing
Diversify carefully across many sectors
quiz.why: Driven by loss aversion — losses hurt about twice as much as equal gains feel good — investors rush to lock in small gains but refuse to realise losses, clinging to losers in the hope of breaking even.
quiz.approach: Base sell decisions on the forward-looking thesis, not on your entry price, and pre-commit exit rules while calm.
